title: What We Carry
content:
We carry mornings we couldn't name
and nights that left their fingerprints on our ribs.
We carry the quiet chores of becoming—
how a small kindness can tilt a whole day,
how grief returns like a tide that knows the shore.

There are rooms we leave the light on for,
conversations we only finish in our heads,
and the simple miracle of breath meeting breath—
someone asking, "How are you?" and waiting for the real answer.

We are not unfinished. We are unfolding.
We are not late. We are learning the clock of our own hearts.
And if today all you carried was yourself
from one moment into the next—
that's still a kind of sun rising.

title: Between Your Name and Mine
content:
We learned to talk in weather—the warm fronts,
your laughter; the storm warning, my silence.
Some evenings we were windows reflecting windows;
some mornings we were a door that opened.

There is a country between your name and mine
where ordinary things become holy:
the cup you set down, the minute that lingers,
the hand that doesn't forget how to return.

If love is a grammar, we are still syntaxing,
still choosing the gentle conjunctions—
and yet, and also, despite, still.
Come sit with me in the soft grammar of us,
where we keep discovering new ways to say home.

title: The Ledger
content:
I kept a ledger of almosts:
calls unsaid, doors unknocked, roads unwalked.
It was a heavy book, and I kept it close,
as if shame were a kind of passport.

But one day I counted backwards—
how every almost taught me a tenderness,
how every no saved a soft portion of me
for a truer yes I hadn't met yet.

I do not forgive the past. I befriend it.
We share a bench. We watch the river.
When it asks if I would change anything,
I say: only the distance I kept from my own heart.

title: Study Break
content:
Some nights the book is heavier than its pages,
and the clock forgets mercy.
You measure your worth in highlighters and margins,
in the slim applause of checkmarks.

Listen: your brain is not a machine—it is a meadow.
It needs sky, and a minute with wind in it,
and a friend who reminds you your name isn't a grade.

Let the problem rest in its own ink.
Let your shoulders remember what ease feels like.
There is a kind of learning that happens
only when you look up and briefly love the world again.

title: When It Piles Up
content:
It doesn't rain for days—it stacks.
Plates in the sink of the soul, emails like hail.
So many small futures asking to be answered.

Start with a square inch of possible:
one breath, a glass of water, a corner cleared.
Call a friend and borrow their horizon.
Let the list forgive you for being human.

Remember: mountains are moved by knees and minutes,
by the humble choreography of now.
You do not have to fix your life.
You only have to love one piece of it long enough to stay.

title: Practice of Light
content:
This is not denial. This is discipline—
to name the small lamps as they appear:
steam rising from a cup, a bird insisting on morning,
your own pulse counting you worthy.

Hope is not a mood; it's a practice.
Like watering plants that haven't bloomed yet,
like saving a seat for someone you haven't met.
Today, let your faith be ordinary and stubborn—
the way the sun keeps showing up for windows.

title: People I Have Been
content:
I have been the loud room and the quiet chair,
the last to leave and the first to worry.
I have been both apology and echo,
and I have learned to bless each version.

If you meet me on a day I am small,
know I am saving space for someone else inside me.
If you meet me on a day I am bright,
know you are seeing the light that others handed me.

We are a relay of kindness, a hand-to-hand dawn.
And even when we forget each other,
we keep the sky lit for the next traveler.

title: After the Storm
content:
You don't have to hurry your mending.
Even the shore negotiates with the sea.
Pick up the pieces that know your name,
leave the ones that belonged to the wave.

One day, you will laugh and not flinch at the echo.
One day, tenderness will knock and you will let it in.
Until then, be a harbor to yourself—
deep enough for rest, shallow enough for light.

title: Apartment Thermostat
content:
The heat clicks on like a promise,
and the plant leans toward a window I forgot to clean.
I burn the toast and butter it anyway;
we call these small mercies breakfast.

Outside, a bus sighs into its morning—
people making a living out of showing up.
I practice it here: feet on floor, cup in hand,
learning again how to belong to an ordinary day.

title: Call Your Friend
content:
Call the one who knows your middle name
and the way you went quiet after August.
Don't rehearse the script. Let the pauses carry their own weight.
Say: I'm okay and also I'm not.

Hear their laugh fold the room into a safer shape.
Remember: you are not a burden. You are a bridge—
made strong by every foot that trusted you across.

title: Borrowed Umbrella
content:
You showed up with an umbrella big enough
for both our forecasts. The rain didn't stop;
we simply learned a softer way to walk through it.

There were no violins, only the pattern
of water forgetting to frighten us.
I keep the umbrella by the door.
Sometimes I open it in the hallway
just to hear our weather again.

title: The Cup in the Sink
content:
I left your cup in the sink for a week,
still fond of the silhouette your mouth made.
The body keeps small museums: ticket stubs,
wrong turns, dishes that didn't need to wait.

One morning I washed it without ceremony.
It felt like telling the truth to a quiet room:
no one applauds, but something becomes lighter.

title: Saltwater Lessons
content:
There are days my eyes are oceans
that forgot their borders. I let them.
Salt is not a failure; it is a teacher.
It keeps the story honest.

When I finish crying I taste the shore—
ordinary, close, mine again.
I rinse my face and think: the world
returns to its color slowly, like a friend.

title: Payday
content:
I buy oranges, new socks, and the kind of soap
that makes the air behave kindly.
I text my mother a picture of dinner
and she texts back three hearts and a recipe.

Happiness is not larger. It is nearer.
It sits in the chair by the window,
waiting for you to notice you are home.

title: Terms of Surrender
content:
I surrender my polished story.
I surrender the version where I am always right.
I keep the part where I learned to listen
with my whole shoulders.

Forgiveness isn't forgetting; it's furniture—
you have to move it around until the room breathes.
Tonight, I make a path wide enough
for us to pass without bruising.

title: Office Hours
content:
The inbox rehearses its small emergencies.
I rehearse a human answer: Hello.
I saw your effort. I know this matters to you.

At lunch I eat an apple by the loading dock
and remember I have a body. A sparrow
makes its case for the afternoon. I agree.
We both go back to our duties softer.

title: Open Book
content:
You read me in pencil first, gentle,
leaving room for edits that didn't punish us.
We learned each other's margins:
where to underline, where to rest the eye.

When the binding creaked, we did not panic.
We held the book together until the story
remembered how to hold itself.

title: Night Drive
content:
We drove the long road that had forgotten its lines,
letting the radio teach us new names for mercy.
There were no revelations, just a map
that stopped demanding we be earlier versions of us.

When we parked, the silence was not a cliff
but a field. We walked in without fear.

title: The Kept Promise
content:
I promised to notice you when nothing glittered.
Tonight the sink is full and your hair is tired.
I put the kettle on and sweep the floor—
not because it's special, but because you are.

This is not fireworks; this is city light
through blinds at 10 p.m.—steady, warm, chosen.

title: Triage
content:
When everything shouts, I whisper: first breath,
then water, then one reachable corner.
I learn to name triage without shame—
not everything breaks, some things bend.

I am not a failure for doing one thing well.
I am a bridge that knows its weight limits.

title: Grammar of Grief
content:
Grief has its tenses: the past perfect
we loved, the present continuous we ache,
the future conditional we might.
I conjugate without fluency, but honestly.

Some days the sentence ends without a period.
Some days I start a new paragraph and go outside.

title: Practice Room
content:
Hope isn't a stage but a practice room—
scales in the morning, a melody that falters,
hands that learn the kindness of repetition.

On the days I play badly, I still show up.
One note at a time becomes a hymn.

title: Inventory of Softness
content:
The neighbor who returns my bin to the curb.
The cashier who remembers my tea.
The friend who says, Text when you land.

I write them down like groceries for winter.
When the world feels sharp, I cook this list
until it feeds me.

title: Letter I Won't Send
content:
I do not want you back. I want back
the version of me that believed I had to be smaller.
She was brave to survive; I am brave to let her rest.

The letter folds itself. The air is lighter.
I walk to the store and buy flowers with my real name.

title: Kitchen Light
content:
You laugh from the next room
and the kitchen light learns how to be golden.
This is the whole poem.

title: Coins for the Meter
content:
I fed the meter of my attention with coins
of sleep and friendship. The reading still took time.
I am learning to budget without starving my joy.

When the meter blinked red I did not panic.
I walked, I looked at trees, I came back ready.

title: Break Room Gospel
content:
We pass around a box of crackers
like communion for the over-scheduled.
Someone tells a joke that keeps the day from snapping.
We are not a team; we are a choir
that practices survival in harmony.

title: Hands Clean
content:
I washed my hands the way the nurse taught me—
palms, backs, between, thumbs, nails, wrists—
then washed the part of my story that kept gripping.

The sink did not judge me. The mirror didn't either.
I left the bathroom lighter, like a promise.

title: Unpack
content:
We unpacked the weekend like a suitcase—
tickets, crumbs, the sweater that kept you warm.
We did not argue about the mess;
we folded the hours into drawers labeled Ours.

Love is housekeeping for time.

title: Sweater Weather
content:
I put on last year's sweater and find
a kindness in the sleeve I didn't know I owned.
Maybe I am getting older. Maybe I am getting softer.
Maybe those are the same thing and it's alright.

title: All the Lamps On
content:
On the hardest day I turned on every lamp.
The house looked surprised and then relieved.
I read one page, sent one text, ate one pear.
It was not heroism. It was electricity.
It was enough.

title: Bench by the River
content:
I sat with the version of me who left too early.
We watched the water continue without us.
She wasn't a villain; she was tired.
I wasn't a fool; I was learning.

We shared a sandwich and called it forgiven.

title: Postscript
content:
After the crying, the room returns my voice
like a borrowed book. I say thank you to the air.
The air says you're welcome by smelling like rain.

title: Second Person Plural
content:
We isn't ownership; it's choreography.
Your morning, my coffee, our couch.
We practice being two and together
until the grammar makes music.

title: Night Shift Mercy
content:
The nurse adjusted the blanket
without waking her patient. No one applauded.
Some kindness occurs out of sight
and still changes the weather.

title: On Time
content:
I missed the deadline and learned the lesson
that doesn't fit in a grade: my body has limits.
Next time I will schedule room for breath.
This time I will schedule room for grace.

title: Customer Service
content:
Thank you for your patience, I say
and mean it for both of us.
The headset blooms into a small planet
where two strangers solve one problem
and leave a little kinder.

title: Bridge Return
content:
You returned my bridge in better shape
than when you found it. You walked across carefully,
then taught me the names of the boards.
Now I can fix more than one river.

title: Garden Bed
content:
I dug a bed for what hurt and planted marigolds.
It isn't that flowers fix anything; they accompany.
All healing needs witnesses that don't interrupt.

title: Blueprints for Morning
content:
Morning lays its blueprints on the kitchen table—
steam, light, something forgiving in the air.
I promise the day I will try to be soft where I was sharp,
present where I was missing, honest even in the small ways.

It is a plain miracle to be ordinary.
To button a shirt without hurrying your own heart.
To eat an apple and taste every step the tree made toward you.
I draw a breath. I sign my name at the bottom of it.

title: The Quiet Between Messages
content:
We live in the quiet between messages—
read receipts, unfinished drafts, a hello left hovering.
I want to learn the economy of gentleness:
to spend less apology and more presence,
less lightning, more lamp.

Come sit with me in the middle of our day.
Let our thumbs rest. Let our voices remember
that patience is a kind of touch you can hear.

title: Inventory
content:
I kept an inventory of what I failed to be.
It grew like a city with no parks.
One afternoon I left the clipboard on a bench
and listened to a fountain tell its whole life to the air.

What if I am not a problem to solve
but a neighborhood to walk—slowly, curiously—
turning corners into surprises,
letting small kindnesses plant their trees.

title: Late Shift
content:
The screen glows like a moon that isn't done talking.
You practice the ancient art of making meaning
out of pages that try to hide it.
If no one told you yet: your effort is a kind of prayer.

Take three minutes to learn the shape of your breath.
This is also study. This is also progress.
You do not have to finish the whole night.
Just finish this minute with mercy.

title: Dishwater Epiphany
content:
In the dishwater I find a galaxy—
small planets of citrus, a comet of foam.
I am so tired of trying to be remarkable.
Let the ordinary astonish me instead.

I rinse a glass until it remembers light.
I stack the bowls like quiet moons.
The evening forgives me for being simple.
I forgive the evening for being brief.

title: The Long Rain
content:
It isn't the storm but the long rain after—
the slow insistence, the careful wearing down.
I want a solution with teeth, but what I get
is a mop, a towel, a neighbor with a joke.

We do not conquer the flood. We accompany the water
until it remembers the river.
If all you did today was keep the lamp dry,
that is still a kind of rescue.

title: Light Maintenance
content:
Hope is not a mood; it is maintenance.
Tighten the screws on the window of your attention.
Oil the hinge of your gratitude until it swings.
Replace the bulb over your doubt with a warmer watt.

You are not naive for trying again.
You are an electrician of your own small dawns.

title: The Table for Two
content:
We are not a miracle. We are a practice—
a table set for two in a house of weather.
Tonight I bring you the day I carried;
you bring me the part of it that loved you back.

Between us: bread, a story, the unafraid truth.
Under us: floorboards learning our weight by heart.
Around us: the long hallway of everything we haven't said
lighting one lamp at a time.

title: Receipt
content:
The heart prints a receipt—
items returned: plans, keys, Sunday afternoons.
No refunds on time. Store credit in lessons.

I keep the paper until it softens in my pocket.
I read it on trains, in waiting rooms, at red lights,
not to hurt myself—just to remember
that I once bought a future and meant it.

title: Salt
content:
There is salt in every good story—
the part where the voice breaks, the part where
we stop pretending we weren't afraid.
Salt keeps the memory honest; it keeps the body real.

Cry if you must. Then drink a glass of water.
Let the ocean you are keep its tides.

title: Staying
content:
I thought love was fireworks. Sometimes it's a porch light
left on because they said they'd be late.
Sometimes it's a ride to the airport at 4 a.m.,
or the last orange saved for you.

Staying isn't spectacle. It's choreography—
a thousand small steps toward the same room.

title: The Net
content:
We built a net out of Tuesdays—
coffee, a meme, a question you really meant.
When I fell, it wasn't the text that caught me
but the years of string we had tied without hurry.

May we keep weaving the ordinary.
May we be foolish enough to believe
that a small net can hold a big sky.

title: Return Policy
content:
I returned the anger for store credit
and bought a gentler sentence:
I did my best with what I knew.
Next time I'll know more.

The clerk nodded as if this happens hourly.
I left the shop lighter, as if regret
were a coat you could learn to wear open.

title: Syllabus for Joy
content:
Week 1: Recess. Week 2: Recess.
Final: A nap you don't apologize for.
Extra credit: Share your snacks with a future self.

Attendance policy: Please arrive exactly as you are.
Late work accepted, especially if it comes
with a story about the sky.

title: Spare Room
content:
I cleaned out a spare room in my chest—
boxed the rage, labeled the sadness, opened a window.
When tenderness knocked, I had somewhere to seat it.
We watched the afternoon learn our names.

Healing is housework, mostly.
The party comes later.

title: Terms and Conditions
content:
I agree to hold your hand under fluorescent lighting,
not just at sunset. I agree to listen
when I'm sure I'm right, and to be surprised.
I agree to leave space for your silences
without packing them with my fear.

You agree to name what hurts before it hardens.
We both agree to eat before arguing.
We initial here: forgiveness in pencil,
because we plan to rewrite it often.

title: Casserole Theology
content:
At the wake someone brings a casserole—
macaroni arguing tenderly with cheese.
Loss is an architecture. Community is the roof.
We do not keep out the rain,
we make a place to stay dry together.

Bless the hands that carry heat
into a room that forgot it could be warm.

title: The Unsent Playlist
content:
I made you a playlist I will never send.
Track one: the day we nearly said the same thing.
Track two: the train we missed and the hour it gave us.
Final track: the question I hum in lowercase.

Not all music is for sharing.
Some of it is just to remind the body
that rhythm was a language before words.

title: Spare Keys
content:
I keep your spare key in the bowl by the door—
not to use it, just to be honest.
Some days I move it to a drawer,
then back again, like weather.

Healing is a choreography of drawers and doors.
One day the key will quietly choose the bin.
I won't throw it away. It will throw me forward.

title: Field Notes on Tears
content:
Day 12: tears during the third commercial.
Day 19: tears at the crosswalk for no reason except traffic.
Conclusion: the body knows when the inbox is full.
Recommendation: archive by water.

Final note: every time I cry, I see farther.
The horizon is a soft instrument.

title: Errand List Mercy
content:
I put mercy on my errand list between stamps and soap.
It wasn't a metaphor—I meant to practice it
at the crosswalk, the checkout, with myself
when I picked the slowest line again.

The day wasn't heroic. It was human
and that was finally enough.

title: Patch Notes for a Heart
content:
v2.1: reduced crash frequency when reminded of autumn.
Fixed a bug where old songs reopen closed rooms.
Improved performance during conversations that require honesty.
Known issue: sunsets still cause soft restarts.

Workaround: breathe, hydrate, text a friend a photo of the sky.

title: Hold Music
content:
On hold for seventeen minutes, I practice
saying my name kindly. The music is an aquarium
no one feeds. A voice assures me how important I am.
I believe it a little.

When a human arrives, I do not spend my patience
like a weapon. We fix a small problem together
and hang up slightly less alone.

title: Rent Week
content:
I count the days on the back of an envelope
and the breath between them. I make pasta stretchy
with advice from the internet and my grandmother.
I say no to three things that wanted my worry
and yes to a walk that wanted my shoulders.

The mailbox does not love me; the sky does
in a way I'm still learning to bank.
